input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware. It comprises lower‑level and upper‑level chassis structures and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception and actuation modules). The vehicle operates in an adaptive cruise‑control (ACC) scenario, using resource‑constrained electronic control units (ECUs) that require off‑loading of compute‑intensive functions, making it a suitable candidate for digital‑twin replication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides three principal services: (1) Data Monitoring – a graphical user interface (UI) displays live vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) runs on cloud‑based hardware within the twin and returns the desired speed to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue an emergency‑brake command through the UI. All services require low end‑to‑end latency communication between the vehicle and the twin and support both operators (monitoring and control) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT requires a clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms between the physical twin’s sensors/actuators and the digital twin components. This limit is imposed by hardware constraints; although safety‑critical vehicular standards may demand tighter bounds, the implemented system targets a maximum 100 ms offset for all time‑critical data exchanges.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture instantiates three core entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on a separate hardware platform within the vehicle. Consequently a single logical DT is composed of one instance of each entity, yielding a hierarchical, entity‑based structure rather than multiple parallel twins; the distribution across hardware components reflects a decentralized placement of functionality while maintaining a single overall twin per vehicle.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, mapping each functional entity (FE) to an individual ROS 2 package that hosts one or more ROS 2 nodes. Communication between the Physical Twin Entity (e.g., point‑cloud clustering & tracking), the Digital Twin Entity (data management and control), and the User Entity (3D visualization and control panel) is orchestrated via ROS 2 middleware, providing entity‑level coordination and resource‑aware deployment across distinct compute units.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates a Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking model (behavioural/analytical) that runs inside the Physical Twin Entity and consumes raw LiDAR data from the onboard Hokuyo UST‑10LX sensor; the resulting object tracks are forwarded to the Data Collection component of the Digital Twin Entity, which stores them in the Data Storage subsystem (implemented as an SQL database). The model is implemented as a ROS 2 node within a ROS 2 package mapped to a Functional Entity. No additional geometric models are described.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation capabilities are encapsulated in the Physical Twin Entity. Functional entities responsible for actuating the Observable Manufacturing Element are realized as ROS2 nodes inside individual ROS2 packages. Remote actuation commands are issued via the User Interface FE (control panel) and transmitted through the Data Collection and Device Control Entity to the underlying hardware actuators. Specific actuator types, ranges, or safety constraints are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR and an Intel Deepsense D435i depth camera mounted on the vehicle; both sensors generate point‑cloud data that is processed by the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking model in the Physical Twin Entity. All devices share a common time base with a required synchronization accuracy of 100 ms to satisfy hardware limitations and safety‑critical timing constraints.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The primary fidelity requirement specified is a time‑synchronization accuracy of 100 ms across all devices, driven by hardware limitations and safety‑critical vehicular application standards. This timing constraint serves as the main quality assurance metric mentioned. No explicit model validation, verification procedures, uncertainty quantification, or error‑correction mechanisms are described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, motor status, steering servo position, and overall vehicle state) is captured and transmitted to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Within the DT, the data streams are routed through a ROS 2 middleware network. The raw LiDAR point cloud is first processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which converts the raw points into tracked object representations. All streams then pass through the Data Translation functional entity (ISO 23247 Data Translation FE), which serialises the messages, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions before they are stored or visualised. The flow is event‑driven by sensor acquisition cycles; specific transmission frequencies are not stated in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the DT (e.g., ACC enable/disable, desired speed set‑point, emergency brake command) are sent back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. The commands are formatted by the Data Translation FE to match the vehicle’s expected message schema and are forwarded to the vehicle’s actuation layer (motor and steering servo) after processing by the ACC Controlling FE. The vehicle provides updated vehicle‑state feedback, which is displayed in the UI, confirming command execution. Authentication, validation, and conflict‑resolution mechanisms are not described in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT uses a two‑layer communication architecture. Intra‑twin software components communicate via a ROS 2 network (publish/subscribe middleware). Inter‑entity communication between the physical twin and the DT occurs over a Wi‑Fi network; the transport layer is UDP as stated in the architecture description. The system is required to provide low end‑to‑end latency for ACC control, but exact latency, bandwidth, reliability or security specifications are not given.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The case study mentions that the ACC functionality can be off‑loaded to “better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud,” indicating that the DT may be hosted on cloud or edge resources. No further details are provided regarding computational or storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, specific deployment models (container, VM, etc.), or availability/reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The case study describes an engineering workflow that begins with the definition of Digital Twin System (DTS) requirements, followed by an architectural design phase that maps identified software components (listed in Table I) onto the ISO 23247 reference‑architecture entities. The mapping groups components into Functional Entities (FEs) and implements each FE as an individual ROS 2 package; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE’s functionality. The resulting software components are deployed on separate hardware platforms corresponding to the Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. This approach organises development according to the ISO 23247 entity‑based structure and uses ROS 2 as the implementation substrate. The documents do not mention explicit quality‑assurance processes, version‑control mechanisms, or formal evolution management beyond this architectural mapping.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The User Entity hosts a User Interface Functional Entity that has been split into a 3‑D Visualization component and a Control Panel component. Through these interfaces, operators can monitor real‑time vehicle state data, generate and view reports (Data Monitoring service), and issue commands to the Physical Twin (Remote Management service). The Digital Twin Entity provides a Computation Offloading service in which the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) subsystem determines the lead vehicle and computes a desired speed, effectively delivering a decision support recommendation to the vehicle controller. Decision support is therefore realised via a combination of data visualisation, report generation, automated speed‑setpoint computation, and remote command execution, although the documents do not specify confidence metrics or explicit decision‑making algorithms.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing. ISO 23247 is organised into four parts (Overview and General Principles, Reference Architecture, Digital Representation of Physical Manufacturing Elements, and Information Exchange). The case study adopts the Part 2 reference architecture, employing the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), the Data Collection and Device Control Entity, the Digital Twin Entity, and the User Entity. Functional Entities derived from this architecture are realised as ROS 2 packages. A notable deviation is the introduction of a Physical Twin Entity, which extends the standard’s OME concept to host general automotive resources such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking. The documents also note that many vehicular safety standards require clock synchronization tighter than 100 ms, whereas the implemented system tolerates a 100 ms accuracy due to hardware constraints, indicating partial rather than full compliance with external timing standards.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The case study notes that the ISO 23247 reference architecture is considered insufficient for safety‑critical automotive digital twins. It recommends that safety be promoted to a first‑class concern by adopting existing automotive safety standards, specifically ISO 26262. While the document highlights the need for further research on security, it does not describe concrete cybersecurity protocols, access‑control mechanisms, authentication methods, or threat‑mitigation strategies.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the physical twin's description, including its scale, chassis structure, and the rationale for using it (resource constraints requiring offloading). It is specific to the text provided. Supported by quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis', and 'off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The extraction provides a detailed and precise list of the three services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with specific capabilities matching the source text. It also correctly identifies the user roles and latency requirements. Supported by quotes: 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports of vehicle state data', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT... determines the lead vehicle, then computes a desired speed', and 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly list specific tools, technologies, and enablers used in the implementation. Section III-B describes the hardware stack (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i) and software tools (NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2), Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink). Table I also mentions the use of a 'SQL database'. Because the extraction claims the information is missing when it is present, the score is 1.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

No relevant evidence is present. II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the Point-Cloud Clustering & Tracking model as a behavioral/analytical model located in the Physical Twin Entity, consuming raw LiDAR data from the Hokuyo UST-10LX. It correctly describes the data flow: tracks are forwarded to the Data Collection component and stored in the Data Storage subsystem (SQL database). It also correctly notes the implementation as a ROS 2 node/package mapped to a Functional Entity. This is highly specific and grounded in the provided sources.

The description accurately captures the system architecture, noting that actuation is handled by ROS2 nodes/packages within the Physical Twin Entity and that commands flow from the Control Panel through the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. It correctly observes that specific actuator types beyond 'motor voltage commands', ranges, and safety constraints are not detailed in the text.

The description correctly identifies the specific sensors (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i) and their output (point-cloud data). It accurately links this data to the Point-Cloud Clustering & Tracking model. The requirement for a common time base with 100 ms accuracy, driven by hardware limitations and safety, is correctly extracted and detailed.

The extraction accurately identifies the 100 ms clock synchronization and network latency as the primary timing constraints/fidelity requirements mentioned, citing hardware limitations and safety-critical standards. It correctly identifies that the provided documents do not describe explicit model validation, verification, or uncertainty quantification procedures.

of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
